title: Baby stars ‘sneeze’ giant rings of gas
html_body:
Today in Science and science: Cancer-sniffing dogs, AI finds new bacterial defenses, and more
Brought to you by Eppendorf & Science Prize for Neurobiology

Now accepting entries for the US$ 25,000
Eppendorf & Science Prize for Neurobiology
Application Deadline June 15, 2026
www.eppendorf.com/prize
View this email in your browser
7 April 2026
Today’s Future News sniffs out a potential new way to screen for lung cancer. But first, catch up on the latest science news, including baby stars’ sneezes and the birth of a big current.
Microbiology  |  Science
Computer models reveal bacterial defense systems
Bacteria have evolved a diverse array of immune systems to ward off viruses known as bacteriophages, often by precisely targeting the invaders’ genetic sequences. Scientists have taken advantage of these antiviral defense mechanisms, repurposing them into powerful biotechnologies like the CRISPR-Cas9 gene editing system.

Hoping to discover even more of these bacterial immune systems, the authors of a new study developed a machine learning model called DefensePredictor , which is designed to identify the genes bacteria use to protect themselves against viruses. While these genes tend to cluster in areas of the genome known as “defense islands,” others are scattered throughout the genome or hitch a ride on mobile elements like plasmids, prophages, and transposons. When the researchers tested DefensePredictor on 69 strains of Escherichia coli , the model predicted hundreds of previously unknown defense systems—dozens of which were experimentally validated.

The authors of a separate study developed several machine learning models , which they used to analyze over 120 million proteins from bacterial genomes. That work revealed hundreds of thousands of potential antiviral gene clusters, most of which had no previously known link to immunity. “Together, these studies reveal that bacterial immunity is far more extensive than previously thought and highlight how such discoveries can inspire powerful biotechnologies,” Science Senior Editor Di Jiang wrote in his summary of the works.
Read the second Science Study
Read the paper
Astronomy  |  The Astrophysical Journal Letters
Baby stars ‘sneeze’ giant rings of gas
An artist’s rendering of a stellar nursery in the Taurus molecular cloud. In the lower right, a baby star’s protostellar disk “sneezes” out a massive ring of gas, which is threaded with magnetic field lines.  Y. Nakamura, K. Tokuda et al.
Baby stars, like human babies, start out in nurseries—albeit ones that are full of interstellar gas and dust rather than comfy cribs. And just like human infants, these young stars sometimes get a little gassy. Scientists studying baby stars have previously observed spike-like structures forming on the dense protostellar disks of gas and dust that rotate around the star. These so-called “sneezes” (would “farts” have been too undignified?) help release excess energy and ensure proper star formation. Now, one team of researchers has discovered a truly enormous sneeze.

The Atacama Large Millimeter Array (ALMA), a set of 66 radio telescopes in the desert of northern Chile, allows researchers to peer through the clouds of gas and dust that make up stellar nurseries—providing a clear view of baby stars. When the authors of a new study used the array to examine the Taurus molecular cloud, they discovered a giant ring-shaped structure about 1000 astronomical units in size near one baby star. This ring, which is made of dense gas, is slightly warmer than its surroundings and was likely created by magnetic field lines penetrating through the protostellar disk.

The findings, lead study author Kazuki Tokuda explained in a statement, suggest that baby stars “undergo dynamic magnetic-gas redistribution shortly after birth, generating shock waves that warm the surrounding gas.”
Read the paper
Earth Science  |  PNAS
Current history, southern edition
The South Pole is home to some of the world’s largest populations of penguins, albatross, and whales. It’s also home to the world’s largest flow of water: the Antarctic Circumpolar Current (ACC), which encircles Antarctica with 100 times more water volume than all of Earth’s rivers combined. But how did this behemoth current start moving?

Scientists have thought that the ACC began when Australia and South America drifted northward 34 million years ago, opening up a channel for all that water to flow. A team decided to look deeper by running climate simulations based on reconstructions of where the continents were and how the oceans were moving at that time. The model revealed that while Australia moving away from Antarctica was crucial, the ACC took time to build up strength . At first, it was only strong through the Atlantic and Indian oceans, not the Pacific. Then, westerly winds intensified the flow, until the current was strong enough to block warm water from reaching the pole. Ice could then accumulate and help cool the planet.

Since the ACC began when carbon dioxide levels in the atmosphere were much higher than today, simulating its origins can help scientists make predictions about a warmer future. “This new knowledge will therefore help us to more reliably interpret recent changes in Southern Ocean circulation,” says co-author Johann Klages in a statement.
Read the paper
SPONSORED
Eppendorf & Science Prize for Neurobiology: Call for Entries 2026
This prize is awarded to young scientists for their outstanding contributions to neurobiological research based on experimental methods of molecular, cellular, systems, or organismic biology. Researchers not older than 35 years are invited to apply.
Apply Now
Future News
Mark Watson via Wikimedia Commons | CC BY
Can dogs be trained to sniff out lung cancer?
Our furry best friends have an extraordinary sense of smell. Canine companions can be trained to sniff out almost anything, from illegal drugs to subtle changes in blood sugar. In recent years, scientists have also investigated dogs’ ability to detect a wide variety of diseases. It turns out these pooches can be trained to identify the odor of urinary tract infections, Parkinson’s disease, chronic wasting disease, and—perhaps most intriguingly—several different types of cancer.

Researchers are particularly interested in finding ways to improve the early detection of lung cancer, which remains the leading cause of cancer-related death worldwide.  Although low-dose computed tomography (LDCT) scans present an effective, non-invasive option for high-risk individuals, these still involve exposure to some radiation and frequently result in false positives. Previous studies suggest trained sniffer dogs can reliably detect lung cancer from urine and breath samples, but such studies tend to vary in terms of quality and sample size.

In a medRxiv preprint, researchers evaluated a newly refined cancer detection procedure known as the Cancer Detection Dog Collective (CDDC®) method . The team used cotton masks to collect breath samples from more than 824 volunteers, including 111 with a confirmed diagnosis of lung cancer. A team of five trained sniffer dogs analyzed the samples, with each dog performing 125 test runs in a facility filled with unmarked, randomly arranged scent boards. While many previous studies have examined results from individual dogs, the CDDC® method only marks a sample as positive if at least three dogs indicate it.

Using this method, the dog team was able to positively identify samples belonging to individuals with cancer more than 95% of the time—with no false positives.

While the method demonstrated high consistency, cloudy weather and the identity of the supervisor responsible for coordinating the search runs both appeared to have an impact on the dogs’ performance. The researchers also note that, because the design of this study was retrospective, future research is required to validate the results. It also remains unclear whether dogs can detect early, preclinical cases of lung cancer with comparable accuracy to cases that have already been diagnosed.

“Even if this dog scent method may not be suitable for the direct early detection of lung cancer as part of a screening program,” the team writes, “it has the potential to reduce the number of false-positive CT findings and to confirm a lung cancer diagnosis.”
Read the preprint
Et Cetera
Can an AI ever forget?
Large language models are supposed to learn from the data they’re trained on, but sometimes, they memorize it instead—and spit it back out, word for word, potentially violating copyright or exposing sensitive information such as credit card numbers and addresses. It’s a poorly understood phenomenon, and one that a new tool—dubbed Hubble—aims to study in detail, in the hopes of uncovering how to ensure AIs forget what they should.
Read more at ScienceInsider
In Iran, universities face increasing fire
As war escalates, universities and research institutions are increasingly experiencing collateral damage and even being targeted by strikes. Even those not in the line of fire are feeling the effects of the fighting. “Graduate students are locked out of labs and dormitories, while widespread internet blackouts have paralyzed research, leaving scholars unable to access email or manage peer-review submissions,” one biologist explained.
Read more at ScienceInsider
Microplastics under the microscope
A new research program announced by President Donald Trump’s administration seeks to investigate microplastic pollution and its health effects, potentially leading to increased regulation. The money may help with key hurdles to understanding the risks of these tiny particles, including “developing clear definitions, ensuring adequate lab capacity, and standardizing sampling and testing methods,” noted one expert.
Read more at News from Science
There was widespread alarm at AI programs encouraging adolescent thoughts of suicide, but it now appears that this might be acceptable if a human therapist acted in the same manner.
—Victor Reus, University of California, San Francisco
STAT News  |  3 April 2026  |  Theresa Gaffney
The U.S. Supreme Court’s 8-1 decision that a Colorado law banning conversion therapy restricts therapists’ free speech could reduce the authority of state medical boards to regulate certain kinds of medical care.
Last but not least
Congrats to the astronauts on Artemis II who have now traveled farther from Earth than any other human being. Hope your trip home is smooth sailing!
Christie Wilcox, Editor, ScienceAdviser

With contributions from Phie Jacobs and Hannah Richter
